title: The saga of nonprofit hospitals losing money and teetering on the brink of bankruptcy is not a new story, but most have usually been rescued in the past by merging or being acquire

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "Nonprofit hospitals often lose money and face bankruptcy, but have historically been rescued through mergers or acquisitions.",
    "Madera Community Hospital struggled financially for years due to the state's underfunding of Medi-Cal reimbursement rates and various government mandates.",
    "St. Agnes Hospital loaned $15.4 million to Madera Community Hospital to help keep it open.",
    "An agreement was reached that included 13 conditions to keep Madera Community Hospital open serving over 150,000 residents.",
    "California law requires the Attorney General to approve agreements between nonprofit health entities.",
    "The Attorney General has the authority to insert conditions into agreements, even if they are unreasonable.",
    "The Attorney General added six new conditions to the agreement between Trinity Health and Madera Community Hospital, making it unworkable.",
    "Madera Community Hospital closed in December 2022 after negotiations failed due to the Attorney General's conditions.",
    "At least two patients died while being transported to other hospitals due to the closure of Madera Community Hospital.",
    "An independent hospital economics expert warned that Madera Community Hospital would likely close without assistance from Trinity Health.",
    "The Attorney General imposed a five-year price cap on medical services and supplies that would have resulted in operating at a loss for the new owner of Madera Community Hospital.",
    "If the agreement had been approved, new operators would have been unable to raise prices on medical services or supplies."
  ]
}
```
